verses.commentary: Then Pilate took Jesus and had Him flogged. Scourging ordered. Phragelloō (φραγελλόω), Roman flogging. Brutal preliminary to crucifixion. Innocence not respected.
The soldiers twisted together a crown of thorns and put it on His head. They clothed Him in a purple robe. Mock coronation. Thorns: painful crown. Purple: royal mockery. Cruel theater.
And went up to Him again and again, saying, Hail, king of the Jews! And they slapped Him in the face. Repeated abuse. Sarcastic homage. Physical assault. Ironic truth in mockery.

Once more Pilate came out and said to the Jews gathered there, Look, I am bringing Him out to you to let you know that I find no basis for a charge against Him. Second declaration of innocence. Pilate presents Jesus. No grounds for charges. But continues trial.

When Jesus came out wearing the crown of thorns and the purple robe, Pilate said to them, Here is the man! Ecce homo. Idou ho anthrōpos (ἰδοὺ ὁ ἄνθρωπος). Behold the man. Son of Man displayed. Human suffering embodied.

As soon as the chief priests and their officials saw Him, they shouted, Crucify! Crucify! But Pilate answered, You take Him and crucify Him. As for me, I find no basis for a charge against Him. Demand for execution. Third innocence declaration. Pilate tries to shift responsibility. Cowardice masquerading as justice.

The Jewish leaders insisted, We have a law, and according to that law He must die, because He claimed to be the Son of God. Theological charge. Blasphemy. Leviticus 24:16. Son of God claim is the issue. Religious grounds for death.
When Pilate heard this, he was even more afraid. New fear. Son of God language terrifies pagan governor. Supernatural dread.
And he went back inside the palace. Where do You come from? But Jesus gave him no answer. Origin question. Silence. No answer given. Isaiah 53:7. Lamb before shearers.
Do You refuse to speak to me? Don't You realize I have power either to free You or to crucify You? Pilate's indignation. Power claim. Authority assertion. Threat.

Jesus answered, You would have no power over Me if it were not given to you from above. Therefore the one who handed Me over to you is guilty of a greater sin. Divine sovereignty. Power from above. God grants authority. Greater guilt: Caiaphas or Judas. Lesser guilt for Pilate. Degrees of culpability.

From then on, Pilate tried to set Jesus free, but the Jewish leaders kept shouting, If you let this man go, you are no friend of Caesar. Anyone who claims to be a king opposes Caesar. Political blackmail. Friend of Caesar: technical title. Loss of favor threatened. Jesus as rival to emperor. Treason charged.

When Pilate heard this, he brought Jesus out and sat down on the judge's seat at a place known as the Stone Pavement (in Aramaic, Gabbatha). Judgment seat. Lithostroton (λιθόστρωτος), stone pavement. Gabbatha. Official verdict location. Public judgment.

It was the day of Preparation of the Passover; it was about noon. Here is your king, Pilate said to the Jews. Chronology. Day before Passover. Sixth hour. Lambs being slaughtered. Ironic presentation. Your king. True King displayed.

But they shouted, Take Him away! Take Him away! Crucify Him! Shall I crucify your king? We have no king but Caesar. Rejection of kingship. No king but Caesar: tragic apostasy. Israel rejects God's anointed. Rome embraced.
Finally Pilate handed Him over to them to be crucified. So the soldiers took charge of Jesus. Pilate capitulates. Surrenders to pressure. Jesus delivered. Soldiers take custody. Cross imminent.

Carrying His own cross, He went out to the place of the Skull (in Aramaic called Golgotha). Jesus bears cross. Isaac carrying wood (Genesis 22). Kranion (κρανίον), skull. Golgotha. Execution site outside city.
There they crucified Him, and with Him two others, one on each side and Jesus in the middle. Crucifixion. Between criminals. Isaiah 53:12. Numbered with transgressors. Central position.

Pilate had a notice prepared and fastened to the cross. It read: Jesus of Nazareth, the King of the Jews. Titlos (τίτλος), title, placard. Crime posted. INRI. Jesus Nazarenus Rex Iudaeorum. Truth proclaimed involuntarily.

Many of the Jews read this sign, for the place where Jesus was crucified was near the city, and the sign was written in Aramaic, Latin and Greek. Trilingual proclamation. Hebrew, Latin, Greek. Universal languages. All world reads. Truth broadcast.

The chief priests of the Jews protested to Pilate, Do not write The King of the Jews, but that this man claimed to be king of the Jews. Objection to title. Want it disclaimered. Claimed to be. Pilate refuses.
Pilate answered, What I have written, I have written. Refusal to change. Gegrapha gegrapha (γέγραφα γέγραφα). Perfect tense. Permanent. Irrevocable. Unwitting truth telling.

When the soldiers crucified Jesus, they took His clothes, dividing them into four shares, one for each of them, with the undergarment remaining. This garment was seamless, woven in one piece from top to bottom. Spoils divided. Four soldiers. Tunic seamless. Valuable garment. High priestly robe echoes (Exodus 28:32).

Let's not tear it. Let's decide by lot who will get it. This happened that the scripture might be fulfilled that said, They divided My clothes among them and cast lots for My garment. So this is what the soldiers did. Gambling for garment. Psalm 22:18 fulfilled. Scripture details prophesied. Providence in smallest acts.

Near the cross of Jesus stood His mother, His mother's sister, Mary the wife of Clopas, and Mary Magdalene. Women at cross. Mary, mother of Jesus. Others present. Faithful witnesses. Men fled; women remain.

When Jesus saw His mother there, and the disciple whom He loved standing nearby, He said to her, Woman, here is your son. Concern for Mary. Beloved disciple (John). New relationship created. Maternal care.
And to the disciple, Here is your mother. From that time on, this disciple took her into his home. Filial responsibility. John receives Mary. Immediate obedience. Household provision. Honor to Mary.

Later, knowing that everything had now been finished, and so that Scripture would be fulfilled, Jesus said, I am thirsty. All accomplished. Scripture fulfillment continues. Psalm 69:21. Physical suffering. Human thirst.

A jar of wine vinegar was there, so they soaked a sponge in it, put the sponge on a stalk of the hyssop plant, and lifted it to Jesus' lips. Sour wine offered. Hyssop: Passover plant (Exodus 12:22). Symbolic connection to sacrifice.

When He had received the drink, Jesus said, It is finished. With that, He bowed His head and gave up His spirit. Tetelestai (τετέλεσται). Perfect tense. Completed. Accomplished. Paid in full. Voluntary death. Spirit released. Mission completed.

Now it was the day of Preparation, and the next day was to be a special Sabbath. Because the Jewish leaders did not want the bodies left on the crosses during the Sabbath, they asked Pilate to have the legs broken and the bodies taken down. High Sabbath. Passover Sabbath. Deuteronomy 21:23. Bodies must not remain. Leg-breaking hastens death.
The soldiers therefore came and broke the legs of the first man who had been crucified with Jesus, and then those of the other. Two criminals. Legs broken. Death accelerated.
But when they came to Jesus and found that He was already dead, they did not break His legs. Already dead. No need to break. Passover lamb unblemished (Exodus 12:46).

Instead, one of the soldiers pierced Jesus' side with a spear, bringing a sudden flow of blood and water. Spear thrust. Confirmation of death. Blood and water. Medical accuracy. Physical and spiritual significance. Cleansing and life.

The man who saw it has given testimony, and his testimony is true. He knows that he tells the truth, and he testifies so that you also may believe. Eyewitness account. John's testimony. Truth claim. Belief purpose. Apologetic intent.

These things happened so that the scripture would be fulfilled: Not one of His bones will be broken. Exodus 12:46, Numbers 9:12, Psalm 34:20. Passover lamb typology. No broken bones. Prophetic fulfillment.
And, as another scripture says, They will look on the One they have pierced. Zechariah 12:10. Piercing prophesied. Looking brings repentance. Future recognition.

Later, Joseph of Arimathea asked Pilate for the body of Jesus. Now Joseph was a disciple of Jesus, but secretly because he feared the Jewish leaders. With Pilate's permission, he came and took the body away. Secret disciple. Fear. Now courage. Requests body. Pilate grants. Burial begins.

He was accompanied by Nicodemus, the man who earlier had visited Jesus at night. Nicodemus brought a mixture of myrrh and aloes, about seventy-five pounds. Nicodemus returns. John 3 connection. Night visitor now day confessor. Royal burial spices. Lavish amount. Honor to King.

Taking Jesus' body, the two of them wrapped it, with the spices, in strips of linen. This was in accordance with Jewish burial customs. Jewish burial. Linen and spices. Proper honor. Hasty but dignified.
At the place where Jesus was crucified, there was a garden, and in the garden a new tomb, in which no one had ever been laid. Garden tomb. Near Golgotha. New tomb. Unused. Honor and purity.
Because it was the Jewish day of Preparation and since the tomb was nearby, they laid Jesus there. Time pressure. Preparation day. Nearby tomb. Convenient. Providential location. Sabbath approaching.
